title: Pollo alla pizzaiola
subtitle: Chicken thighs in tomato, oregano, and caper sauce
excerpt: Pizzaiola means pizza-maker's style: tomato, oregano, garlic, and olives — the flavours of Neapolitan pizza applied to braised chicken thighs. It takes 40 minutes, produces a deeply savoury sauce, and improves considerably when made a day ahead.
sourceNotes: Alla pizzaiola is a Neapolitan preparation documented from the late nineteenth century, originally applied to thin-cut beef or pork but extended throughout southern Italy to chicken. Artusi includes pizzaiola preparations in La scienza in cucina (1891, public domain) in his discussion of Neapolitan meat dishes. The combination of tomato, oregano, capers, and olives is characteristic of the Campanian coast and appears across multiple proteins and preparations.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freezes well for up to 3 months. Thaw overnight and reheat gently.
recipe.batchNotes: Double the recipe and freeze half.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The dish improves overnight. Make it the day before and refrigerate; the flavours deepen significantly.

content.text: Pizzaiola is a Neapolitan preparation that takes the flavour profile of their most famous export and applies it to meat. Tomato, oregano, garlic, capers, and black olives: it is not a subtle combination, but that is not the point. The point is a deeply savoury, salty-acidic sauce that the chicken slowly absorbs as it braises, until the meat and sauce become inseparable.

Chicken thighs work far better than breast here. They are fatty enough to braise without drying out, and they release gelatin into the sauce as they cook, thickening it naturally. Brown them properly first — pale, steamed chicken going into a sauce produces a thin result.

Pat the {{chicken-thigh}} dry and season with salt and pepper. Warm the {{olive-oil}} of olive oil in a wide, heavy casserole over medium-high heat. Place the thighs skin-side down and cook without moving for 7 to 8 minutes until the skin is deep golden and releases easily from the pan. Turn and cook for 3 more minutes on the other side. Remove and set aside. Pour off all but 1 tablespoon of fat from the pan.

Reduce the heat to medium. Add the {{garlic}} of garlic to the pan and cook for 1 minute until pale gold. Pour in the {{white-wine-dry}} of white wine and scrape up any browned bits. Let bubble for 1 minute. Add the {{tinned-tomatoes}} of tomatoes, the {{oregano-dried}} of oregano, the {{capers}} of capers, and the {{olives-black}} of olives. Stir to combine.

Return the chicken thighs to the pan, skin-side up, nestled into the sauce. Bring to a simmer, cover, and cook over low heat for 25 minutes until the meat is cooked through and tender. Remove the lid and cook for 5 more minutes to concentrate the sauce slightly. Taste for seasoning — the capers and olives add salt, so taste before adding any. Rest for 5 minutes before serving. Good with crusty bread, polenta, or steamed rice.

items.cause: Capers and olives not rinsed, or salt added without tasting
items.fix: Rinse capers under cold water; always taste before seasoning; the sauce has natural saltiness from the olives and capers

items.symptom: Chicken skin is soft and pale rather than golden
items.cause: Pan not hot enough, or skin not dried before cooking
items.fix: Pat the chicken completely dry; make sure the oil is hot and shimmering before the chicken goes in; do not move it for the full 7 minutes

items.cause: Lid left on the whole time, trapping steam; or too much wine not reduced first
items.fix: Remove the lid for the final 5 minutes to let the sauce reduce; it should coat the back of a spoon

items.symptom: Oregano tastes dusty rather than aromatic
items.cause: Old dried oregano that has lost its volatile oils
items.fix: Use dried oregano within 12 months of opening; rub it between your palms before adding to release the oils

content.text: Pizzaiola as a preparation is inseparable from Naples and Campania. The name references the pizzaiolo, the pizza maker, and the sauce uses the same flavour logic as Neapolitan pizza: ripe tomato, dried oregano, garlic, and olive oil in simple proportion, without elaboration. The preparation extends throughout southern Italy and into the diaspora, where it appears across Italian-American cooking as a quick weeknight preparation. It is one of the reliably transportable flavour ideas of southern Italian cuisine: a set of relationships (acid, salt, herb, oil) that works across many proteins and contexts.
